ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for piccata alla milanese. FOODON_00002764 (chicken dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (chicken meat, cooked) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for piccata alla milanese as a composite dish with pasta.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for piccata alla milanese or sufficiently close composite Italian chicken-pasta dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of piccata alla milanese consists of approximately 400 g, comprising one breaded chicken breast fillet (approximately 150 g) and a portion of spaghetti in pomodoro sauce (approximately 250 g).

nutrient_source.title: Composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central: Chicken breast, cooked (FDC ID 171477); Spaghetti, cooked (FDC ID 168936); Tomato sauce (FDC ID 170501); Egg, whole, cooked (FDC ID 173424); Parmesan cheese (FDC ID 171242)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for piccata alla milanese as a composite dish. Values are estimated by weighted combination of approved USDA FoodData Central entries for the component ingredients at representative proportions: chicken breast cooked ~37.5%, spaghetti cooked ~45%, tomato sauce ~12%, egg ~3%, Parmesan ~2.5%. Energy cross-checked against macro sum (Atwater factors).

gi_source.title: Spaghetti, white, boiled — GI 45 (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, 2008, International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: Piccata alla milanese contains spaghetti as the dominant carbohydrate source. GI of 45 is assigned based on the University of Sydney GI Database value for white spaghetti, boiled (GI 45, low). The breaded chicken component contributes minimal glycemic carbohydrate. Glycemic load per 400 g serving calculated as: (45 × 70 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 31.5. Available carbohydrate per serving = 17.5 g per 100 g × 400 g / 100 = 70 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from Parmesan cheese, tomato sauce, and cooking salt
hypertension.guidance: A 400 g serving provides approximately 840 mg sodium, representing 36–42% of the recommended daily limit of 2000–2300 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from spaghetti portion
type2_diabetes.guidance: The spaghetti component contributes a glycemic load of approximately 31.5 per full serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a half-portion of pasta or substitute with a lower-GI whole-wheat spaghetti to reduce postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean protein from chicken breast with moderate saturated fat from egg and Parmesan
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat is approximately 4.8 g per 400 g serving, within acceptable limits for cardiovascular health; the lean chicken breast and olive oil base support a heart-healthy profile when consumed in moderate portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Piccata alla Milanese is a classic Italian dish originating from Milan, Lombardy, consisting of a chicken breast fillet dipped in beaten egg and coated with grated Parmesan cheese before being pan-fried in olive oil until golden and crisp. It is served alongside spaghetti dressed in a simple pomodoro (tomato) sauce. The chicken is tender and moist inside with a savoury, lightly cheesy crust, while the spaghetti provides a mild, slightly acidic tomato-flavoured carbohydrate base. The dish is a staple of traditional Milanese home cooking and Italian restaurant menus worldwide. Nutritionally, it provides a balanced combination of lean protein from the chicken breast (approximately 11 g per 100 g), moderate carbohydrate from the spaghetti (approximately 17.5 g per 100 g), and relatively low fat (approximately 3.8 g per 100 g). The glycemic index is low (GI 45), driven by the spaghetti component, though the glycemic load per full serving is moderate at approximately 31.5. Sodium content is moderate due to Parmesan and tomato sauce; individuals managing hypertension or Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size. The dish contains gluten (pasta), egg, and dairy (Parmesan) allergens and is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with gluten or dairy intolerances.

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB, USDA, ICMR-NIN) contains a direct entry for piccata alla milanese as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted combination of USDA FoodData Central component entries at representative proportions. GI is assigned by analogy to white spaghetti boiled (GI 45) from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the dominant glycemic carbohydrate source. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are individually high-quality USDA Foundation Foods entries and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component entries used for this composite estimate. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g; this is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for the cooked chicken breast, spaghetti, and tomato sauce components — no industrial trans fats are present in this traditional preparation. Nutrient values represent a composite estimate for the full dish (chicken piccata + spaghetti pomodoro) at the proportions described in nutrient_source. Restaurant portion sizes may vary; the 400 g serving size is a representative estimate for a standard Italian restaurant main course. The food_group has been corrected from the input value of 'Grains and staples' to 'Mixed' as this is a composite dish containing both protein (chicken) and carbohydrate (pasta) components. GI source is analogous estimate based on spaghetti white boiled GI 45 from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables.
